sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: 14.7g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a fruit, sugar moderation is the whole game.
sections.subtitleText: 14.7g of fat. omega 3 is doing a lot of the work here. for a fruit, sugar moderation is the whole game.

sections.title: Carbs
sections.narration: 8.5g of carbs. fibre is doing a lot of the work here. for fruit, the real question is whether the sweetness stays under control.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: 2g of protein. bioavailability is one of the best parts of the protein story. for a fruit, you want sweetness to be kept in check.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin c at 11% daily value, vitamin a at 2% daily value stand out most. that is barely moving the needle.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: minerals are basically not part of the case here.
sections.subtitleText: minerals are basically not part of the case here.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: on the plus side: a true outlier for fruit fat quality because this behaves nothing like typical sweet fruit and that makes the category result exceptional. easy to use as a whole food because simple, recognisable, and realistic for everyday eating. brings a clean fruit identity because it still contributes something valuable even when it is not elite.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: the drawbacks next: calorie density is much higher than most fruits because still useful, but it changes how casually you can throw it in. sugar can climb fast because a lot of fruits look healthy but still lean sweet. satiety is not especially strong because fruit often needs help from the rest of the meal.

closing.summary: Avocado has a genuinely strong overall case. the practical downsides are real, but they do not outweigh the upside. best when you want sweetness that still earns its place nutritionally.
closing.useCaseNote: Best when you want sweetness that still earns its place nutritionally.
closing.cta: Would you rank it the same, or nah?
